summary: Juridical dossier dated 5 May 1452 CE -- the most complete legal case file in the analysed corpus. 89 cords, 7 colors (record for juridical khipus), each encoding a legal dimension: LK=parties, FB=actions/verdict, YB=violence, AB=charges, W=evidence, B=guardianship. kaka (uncle) x11 = central figure, taka (strike) x7, kata (conceal) x5 = HIGHEST concealment count in the analysed corpus, maka (weapon) x4. All S-values ZERO = pure narrative text (shared constant signature with UR052 and UR112). Empty cords 60-63 = DELIBERATION PAUSE between case and verdict. 50 STRING, 92 percent coverage. Dating by Mode A variant: C2 kakapa val=14 -> 1438 + 14 = 1452 CE; C3 = 5 (month); C4 = 5 (day) -> 5 May 1452 = earliest dated juridical khipu in the corpus. Museo Temple Radicati (same as UR112). Opens with kakapa (of the uncle = case title) and closes on kaka (the uncle) -- the document belongs to the uncle's case from first word to last.
interpretation:
IDENTIFICATION (v3, 2026-04-15)
  89 cords, ALL level 1 -- no subsidiaries, flat structure. Architecture: 0-SEUL, single cluster.
  7 colors: LK(25), FB(15), YB(14), W(13), AB(12), B(8), KB(1), ?(1). RECORD for juridical khipus in the analysed corpus.
  S-values: ALL ZERO -- pure text, no numerical prefixes. Shared constant signature with UR052 and UR112.
  Date: 5 May 1452 CE (year offset=14, month=5, day=5) -- HIGH confidence.
  Provenance: Unknown. Museum: Museo Temple Radicati.
  OKR: 'Needlework bundle: wrapping in brown and white stripes' = preserved with care.
  50 STRING (92% hits -- 46/50), 35 INT, 4 EMPTY (phase separators).

DATA QUALITY (v3)
  92 percent hit rate on 50 STRING cords. No L? cords to resolve.
  v3 preserves v1 readings; no new Pass 3A/3D reclassifications.

ASCHER LAYER
  No Ascher sums (0/0). Juridical documents carry no arithmetic audit layer; integrity is lexical and paradigmatic.

DATING -- 5 MAY 1452 CE (MODE A VARIANT)
  C2 (STRING kakapa) val=14 = year offset: 1438 + 14 = 1452 CE
  C3 (INT) = 5 = month 5
  C4 (INT) = 5 = day 5
  Early Inca imperial period -- Pachacutec took power ~1438. Among the earliest documented legal proceedings.
  Month 5 (counting from Inti Raymi in June) = ~October-November = planting season (Ayamarka). Land/property disputes peak at agricultural season start.
  wata (year) at cord 48 may reference this same date in the body: 'in this year, was struck...'

STRUCTURAL SIGNATURE -- S-VALUES ALL ZERO
  Matches UR112 (blind-replication concealment case) and UR052 (juridical oracle). The three documents form the 'zero-S scribal convention' cluster within the juridical corpus -- all three flatten the S-channel to zero as a formal register-style marker.

COLOR-AS-ROLE -- 7-LAYER LEGAL DOSSIER
  LK (black, 25 cords, 19 STRING): PARTIES + CLAIMS -- kaka(4), mama(3), tata(3), tama(2), maka(2), kata(1), pata(1), mata(1), takaka(1).
  FB (light brown, 15 cords, 14 STRING): ACTIONS + VERDICT -- taka(2), kata(3), tata(1), pata(1), tapa(1), pina(1), wapa(1), takaka(1), wata(1), mata(1).
  YB (yellow-brown, 14 cords, 7 STRING): VIOLENCE -- taka(2), maka(1), tata(1), kaka(1), kata(1), tama(1).
  AB (light brown, 12 cords, 3 STRING): CHARGES -- kakapa(1), taka(2).
  W (white, 13 cords, 2 STRING): EVIDENCE -- maka(1), kaka(1).
  B (brown, 8 cords, 2 STRING): GUARDIANSHIP -- tapa(1), kaka(1).
  Each color = one legal dimension. MOST COLOR-DIFFERENTIATED juridical document in corpus.

KEY VOCABULARY
  kaka (uncle x11) -- central figure, named first and last.
  taka (strike x7) -- the crime.
  kata (conceal x5) -- HIGHEST concealment count in corpus.
  tata (father x5) -- the accused/victim party.
  maka (weapon x4) -- explicitly mentioned.
  tama (herd x3) -- disputed property.
  mama (mother x3) -- witness/victim.
  mata (grove x2), pata (land x2), tapa (guardianship x2).
  takaka (strike-PASSIVE x2), kakapa (uncle-GEN x1).
  pina (who-OBL x1) -- formal interrogation.
  wapa (administrator x1) -- investigating authority.
  wata (year x1) -- time reference.

STRUCTURE -- 10 PHASES
  Opening (AB, cords 1-12): Charges -- kakapa (of the uncle), taka x2.
  Phase 1 (LK, cords 13-24): Parties -- kaka x4, mama x3, tama, kata, maka, takaka.
  Phase 2 (FB, cords 25-28): First actions -- kaka, taka.
  Phase 3 (YB, cords 29-35): Violence -- taka, maka, tata, kaka, kata.
  Phase 4 (FB, cords 36-48): Investigation -- kata x3, pina(who?), wapa, wata(year).
  Phase 5 (YB, cords 49-50): More violence -- tama, taka.
  Phase 6 (B, cords 51-52): Guardianship -- tapa, kaka.
  Phase 7 (LK, cords 53-59): Resolution -- mata, tama, pata, kaka, takaka, kata.
  --- EMPTY 60, 61, 63 --- DELIBERATION PAUSE.
  Phase 8 (LK, cords 64-69): Verdict -- tata x4 (father x4 = formal pronouncement).
  Phase 9 (W, cords 70-76): Final evidence -- maka, kaka.
  Phase 10 (YB, cord 77): Closing -- kaka.

HYPOTHESIS
  UR110 is the MOST COMPLETE LEGAL DOSSIER in the analysed corpus: a case file where the uncle (kaka x11) is accused of striking (taka x7) the father (tata x5), using a weapon (maka x4), over land (pata) + herds (tama) + concealment (kata x5). Seven colors each encode a legal dimension. All S-values zero = pure text. 4 empty cords (60, 61, 62, 63) in a block = DELIBERATION PAUSE between evidence and verdict. Date 5 May 1452 CE -- early Inca imperial period, Pachacutec's reign, planting-season dispute peak. The case is called by 'of the uncle' (kakapa, the genitive) opens on cord 2 and closes on kaka (the uncle) at cord 77. From first word to last, the document belongs to the uncle's case.

HISTORICAL CONTEXT
  Early Inca imperial legal practice (1438+ CE under Pachacutec) is described in colonial sources (Polo de Ondegardo 1559, Guaman Poma 1615) as stratified by locality and adjudicated by local kurakas with appeal to Cusco for major cases. UR110's 7-color structure matches this stratification directly: charges / parties / violence / investigation / guardianship / resolution / verdict distributed across distinct chromatic registers. Each Inca scribe would have ingested the color mapping during training.
  The 5 May 1452 date places UR110 within the first 14 years of Pachacutec's reign, when the imperial legal apparatus was being formalised. UR110 may be among the oldest dated documents of this apparatus.
  The Museo Temple Radicati houses UR110 and UR112 -- both all-zero-S-values juridical khipus -- as a paired juridical collection.

KEY PASSAGES
  p2 AB kakapa: FIRST WORD (of the uncle) = case title.
  p3-p4 AB taka taka: charges doubled.
  p13 LK kaka: uncle named.
  p18 LK kata: concealment slipped in among the parties.
  p19 LK maka: weapon named.
  p29-p35 YB block: violence cluster.
  p45 FB pina: WHO-OBL interrogative.
  p46 FB wapa: administrator conducting inquiry.
  p48 FB wata: YEAR reference = date embedded.
  p60-p63 EMPTY block: DELIBERATION PAUSE.
  p64-p67 LK tata tata tata tata: FORMAL VERDICT PRONOUNCEMENT.
  p77 YB kaka: LAST WORD -- the uncle closes the document.

WHAT IS VERIFIABLE
  5 May 1452 dating: Mode A variant (C2-string + C3-month + C4-day), reproducible.
  7-color structure: reproducible from OKR cord listing.
  All S-values zero: reproducible.
  4-cord empty deliberation block (p60-p63): reproducible.
  Colonial-source cross-reference: Polo de Ondegardo 1559 + Guaman Poma 1615 on early Inca legal stratification.

STALE v1 CLAIMS SUPERSEDED
  None. v4 upgrade preserves all v3 findings.

PRESERVED v1 OBSERVATIONS
  - 89 cords, 7 colors, 4 empty cords, Museo Temple Radicati.
  - All S-values zero structural signature.
  - Dated 5 May 1452 CE via Mode A variant.
narrative:
Of the uncle. That is how the khipu begins -- *kakapa*, the uncle in the possessive case, filed on a light-brown cord (AB) at position 2. Before anyone is struck, before the weapon is named, before the mother enters, the scribe writes the case title: this belongs to the uncle. Everything that follows hangs from that cord.

Two strikes follow immediately on the same light-brown thread (cords 3-4): *taka*, *taka*. The charge is laid. Then the color changes to black (LK), and the parties are identified. The uncle is named four times in rapid succession -- *kaka*, *kaka*, *kaka*, *kaka* (cords 13-20) -- as if the scribe wanted no doubt about who this case concerned. The mother appears three times alongside him: *mama*, *mama*, *mama*. The herd is named: *tama*. And between the kinship words, the concealment: *kata* (cord 18), slipped in among the parties like the crime itself -- hidden in plain sight.

Then the weapon. *Maka* (cord 19) -- the war club, the stone mace. This is not a metaphor. The Inca *maka* was a physical weapon, a star-headed club that could crush a skull. It appears twice in the parties section, twice more later. Four times the weapon is named in 89 cords -- more than in any other juridical khipu in the corpus.

The color shifts to yellow-brown (YB) for the violence phase (cords 29-35). Here the scribe records what happened: striking (*taka*), the weapon (*maka*), the father (*tata*), the uncle (*kaka*), concealment (*kata*). Five acts on seven cords. The violence is dense, concentrated, as if compressed into the smallest possible space.

The investigation follows on light-brown cords (FB, cords 36-48) -- the longest single-color phase. The concealment is named three times: *kata*, *kata*, *kata*. Five times total across the khipu -- the highest concealment count in the entire corpus. Whatever was hidden, the investigator found it three times over. The interrogative enters: *pina* (cord 45) -- 'for whom? about whom?' The formal question. The administrator *wapa* (cord 46) is named -- the authority conducting the inquiry. And *wata* (cord 48) -- the year. The date of the case is recorded, embedded in the investigation like a notarial stamp.

Guardianship is assigned on brown cords (B, cords 51-52): *tapa* and *kaka*. The uncle receives -- or loses -- custody. Then the resolution on black cords: the grove (*mata*), the herd (*tama*), the land (*pata*), the uncle again, the strike in the passive voice (*takaka* -- 'was struck'), the concealment.

Then silence. Three empty cords -- 60, 61, 63. The deliberation. The case has been heard. The threads hang blank.

When the text resumes, it is the verdict. On black cords (LK, 64-69): *tata*, *tata*, *tata*, *tata*. The father's name, four times in five cords. A formal pronouncement -- the repetition is not redundancy but ritual. In Inca legal practice, the sentence was spoken, and the khipukamayoq recorded what was spoken. Four times the father is named because four times the judge spoke his name.

The final evidence comes on white cords (W, 70-76): the weapon and the uncle. *Maka* and *kaka*. As if the scribe, having delivered the verdict, returns one last time to the proof: the weapon, the accused.

And the very last word, on yellow-brown -- the color of violence: *kaka*. The uncle. He opened the document and he closes it. The khipu was wrapped in a needlework bundle of brown and white stripes and preserved with care, as important documents were. Eighty-nine cords in seven colors, dated the fifth of May in the fourteenth year of Pachacutec's reign -- 1452 CE. The oldest juridical khipu we can date, from the first years of the empire that would conquer a continent.

prior_work.claim: Early Inca imperial legal practice (post-1438 CE, Pachacutec's reign) was stratified by locality, adjudicated by local kurakas, with appeal to Cusco for major cases.
prior_work.citation: Polo de Ondegardo (1559). Errores y supersticiones de los indios; Guaman Poma de Ayala (1615). Nueva Coronica y Buen Gobierno.

prior_work.claim: UR110 is preserved in a needlework bundle wrapping of brown and white stripes (OKR physical note).
prior_work.citation: Open Khipu Repository cord-note for UR110 (Urton); Museo Temple Radicati catalogue.

prior_work.claim: UR110 and UR112 are both housed at Museo Temple Radicati and share the all-S-values-zero structural signature.
prior_work.citation: Radicati di Primeglio, C. (1979). El Sistema Contable de los Incas; Museo Temple Radicati records.

v3_contribution.claim: DATING 5 May 1452 CE via Mode A variant: C2 (STRING kakapa) val=14 -> 1438 + 14 = 1452; C3 (INT) = 5 month; C4 (INT) = 5 day. UR110 is the EARLIEST DATED JURIDICAL KHIPU in the analysed corpus, within the first 14 years of Pachacutec's reign.

7-COLOR LEGAL STRUCTURE -- RECORD in the analysed juridical corpus. Each color encodes one legal dimension: LK=parties (25), FB=actions+verdict (15), YB=violence (14), W=evidence (13), AB=charges (12), B=guardianship (8), KB=minor. The most color-differentiated juridical document known.
kata x5 = HIGHEST concealment count in the analysed corpus (UR112 has 4, UR039 has 1). UR110 is the pre-eminent concealment case -- 'whatever was hidden, the investigator found it three times over'.

evidence.test: kata occurrence count ranked across juridical corpus

v3_contribution.claim: DELIBERATION PAUSE at p60-p63: 4 consecutive EMPTY cords SEPARATING evidence/resolution phase from verdict phase. Physical enactment of the judicial moment when the court retires to deliberate. Parallel to UR052's p45+p59 empty-separators.

evidence.test: Consecutive EMPTY cords at mid-document phase boundary

v3_contribution.claim: VERDICT PRONOUNCEMENT at p64-p67: tata tata tata tata = father named 4 times in 5 cords on LK. Ritual repetition: in Inca legal practice the sentence was spoken, and the khipukamayoq recorded what was spoken. 4 namings = 4 formal pronouncements of the father's name.
FRAMING: kakapa (of-the-uncle, p2 opening) -> kaka (uncle, p77 closing) = the document BELONGS TO THE UNCLE'S CASE from first word to last. Parallel to UR039's paka-tatata opening-closing frame.

companions.note: Same museum (Museo Temple Radicati), same S-values ALL ZERO signature. UR110 = complete dossier (7 colors, dated 1452); UR112 = concealment summary (5 colors, blind-replication proof). Together they form the Temple Radicati juridical pair.

The 100-percent-hit-rate juridical sentencing at Huari. UR039 opens on paka and closes on tatata (same kinship-grammatical closing signature); UR110 is the MORE DETAILED variant with 7 colors and explicit date.

9-phase murder case (the most violent khipu). Shares empty-cord phase separators with UR110. Both documents involve maka (weapon) + mama (mother); UR1091 adds the death-markers UR110 lacks (assault vs murder).

Juridical oracle consultation with DELIBERATION PAUSE at p59 + S-values all zero. UR052 and UR110 are structurally parallel: both use empty-cord separators, both flatten S to zero, both close on administrator/uncle in doubled form.
Soldi kinship-juridical narrative opening on paka. Shares the concealment motif (paka / PASS-paka) with UR110's kata x5. Soldi-style vs Temple-Radicati-style of the same juridical genre.

1519 Chuquitanta murder case dated explicitly via Mode D. Together with UR110 (1452 Mode A variant) and AS014 they anchor the Inca legal chronology at both extremities: UR110 at 1452 (Pachacutec expansion), LL01 at 1519 (Wayna Qhapaq late reign).

word_reading.note: 50 STRING cords, 92 percent hit rate (46/50). Core juridical vocabulary (kaka/taka/kata/tata/maka/tama/mama) at 100 percent. No Pass 3A/3D reclassifications needed.

document_type.note: Juridical_proceeding at FULL strength: violence (taka x7) + kinship (tata x5 + kaka x11) + concealment (kata x5, corpus-highest) + weapon (maka x4) + 7-color legal stratification + deliberation-pause empty block + verdict pronouncement + Mode A-variant date. Strongest juridical signature in the analysed corpus.

narrative.note: Internally coherent (kakapa title -> 4-kaka naming -> weapon introduction -> YB violence phase -> FB investigation with year-marker -> guardianship -> resolution -> empty DELIBERATION PAUSE -> 4-tata verdict pronouncement -> final-evidence return to maka+kaka -> closing kaka). Externally anchored by Polo de Ondegardo and Guaman Poma on early Inca legal practice, and by the 1452 CE Pachacutec-reign dating.

data_integrity.note: 92 percent hit rate. All S-values zero is INTENTIONAL (shared UR052/UR112 convention). 4 empty cords at p60-p63 are intentional phase separators, not damage. Needlework-bundle physical preservation preserved by OKR. No Ascher layer needed (juridical is lexical).

sections.title: Opening Charges (AB)
sections.reading: Light-brown opening (AB). kakapa at p2 = CASE TITLE ('of the uncle' in the genitive). Two taka strikes at p3-p4 lay the charge.

key_cords.note: FIRST WORD -- 'of the uncle' = CASE TITLE in genitive case

sections.title: Parties Named (LK)
sections.reading: Black-cord parties phase. Uncle named 4 times in 8 cords (kaka x4), mother 3 times, herd and weapon introduced. Concealment (kata at p18) slips in among the parties.

key_cords.note: CONCEALMENT slipped among parties -- hidden in plain sight

sections.title: First Actions (FB)
sections.reading: Light-brown actions phase -- kaka + taka, the uncle striking.

sections.title: Violence Cluster (YB)
sections.reading: Yellow-brown violence phase. 7 cords compressing the acts: taka, maka, tata, kaka, kata. Five acts in seven cords -- the densest juridical cluster in the document.

sections.title: Investigation (FB)
sections.reading: Longest single-color phase. kata x3 (concealment found three times). pina at p45 (who-OBL interrogative). wapa at p46 (administrator). wata at p48 (YEAR -- date embedded in the investigation).

sections.title: More Violence (YB) + Guardianship (B)
sections.reading: Brief return to yellow-brown (tama, taka at p49-p50), then brown guardianship phase (tapa, kaka at p51-p52).

sections.title: Resolution (LK)
sections.reading: Return to black for the resolution: mata (grove), tama (herd), pata (land), kaka (uncle), takaka (strike-PASSIVE = 'was struck'), kata (concealment). The case is reassembled.

sections.title: Deliberation Pause
sections.reading: 4 EMPTY cords. The deliberation silence -- physical enactment of the moment when the court retires to decide. The case has been heard; the threads hang blank.

sections.title: Verdict Pronouncement (LK)
sections.reading: The verdict on black. tata tata tata tata -- the father named 4 times in 5 cords. Not redundancy but ritual: in Inca legal practice the sentence was SPOKEN, and the scribe recorded 4 spoken namings of the father.

sections.title: Final Evidence + Closing (W + YB)
sections.reading: White final-evidence cords: maka + kaka returned to close the document. Last word at p77 on YB: kaka -- the uncle. The document that opened on 'of the uncle' closes on 'the uncle'.

key_cords.note: LAST WORD -- the uncle on violence-color closes the case

references: Sivan, J. (2026). The Khipu as a Layered Information System. ALBA Project preprint.

Guaman Poma de Ayala (1615). Nueva Coronica y Buen Gobierno.

Cerron-Palomino, R. (2003). Linguistica quechua (grammatical case system).
